elements.sent-2411-001: Controlling mixed platoons, which consist of both connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) and human-driven vehicles (HDVs), poses significant challenges due to the uncertain and unknown human driving behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-002: Data-driven control methods offer promising solutions by leveraging available trajectory data, but their performance can be compromised by process noise and adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this issue, this paper proposes a Robust Data-EnablEd Predictive Leading Cruise Control (\method{RDeeP-LCC ) framework based on data-driven reachability analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-004: The framework over-approximates system dynamics under noise and attack using a matrix zonotope set derived from data, and develops a stabilizing feedback control law.
elements.sent-2411-005: By decoupling the mixed platoon system into nominal and error components, we employ data-driven reachability sets to recursively compute error reachable sets that account for noise and attacks, and obtain tightened safety constraints of the nominal system.
elements.sent-2411-006: This leads to a robust data-driven predictive control framework, solved in a tube-based control manner.
elements.sent-2411-007: Numerical simulations and human-in-the-loop experiments validate that the \method{RDeeP-LCC method significantly enhances the robustness of mixed platoons, improving mixed traffic stability and safety against practical noise and attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Among these innovations, adaptive cruise control (ACC) has emerged as a significant implementation, reducing the need for constant driver intervention in speed management~\cite{de2014effects and enhancing proactive driving safety~\cite{luo2015intelligent .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Despite these benefits, recent empirical and experimental studies~\cite{gunter2020commercially,makridis2020empirical have revealed the inherent limitations of ACC in optimizing traffic flow.}
elements.sent-2411-010: These limitations are primarily attributed to ACC's over-conservative car-following policy and short-sighted perception capabilities~\cite{shen2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In contrast to ACC, cooperative adaptive cruise control (CACC) employs vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) communication to organize multiple CAVs as a pure CAV platoon and apply cooperative control methods.
elements.sent-2411-012: This approach shows substantial potential in improving traffic performance, including traffic stability~\cite{oncu2014cooperative , road capacity~\cite{smith2020improving , and energy efficiency~\cite{ma2019predictive .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the effectiveness of CACC is hindered by the requirement for all the involved vehicles to possess autonomous capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-014: A mixed traffic environment, characterized by the coexistence of CAVs and human-driven vehicles (HDVs), is anticipated to persist for an extended period.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the near future, particularly at low CAV penetration rates, the probability of consecutive vehicles being equipped with CAV technology becomes negligible~\cite{hajdu2019robust,jiang2023platoon .}
elements.sent-2411-016: To overcome the limitations of pure CAV platoons in mixed traffic, mixed platooning has emerged as a promising alternative, which integrates both CAVs and HDVs in a vehicle platoon~\cite{jin2016optimal,chen2021mixed,yang2022eco .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The core idea is to guide the behavior of HDVs by directly controlling CAVs, thereby enhancing overall traffic performance~\cite{stern2018dissipation,wang2020controllability .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent studies, including traffic simulations~\cite{wu2021flow,zhan2022data , hardware-in-the-loop tests~\cite{guo2022distributed,wang2023implementation , and real-world experiments~\cite{stern2018dissipation,jin2018experimental , have shown the potential benefits of mixed platoons for smoothing traffic flow and improving traffic efficiency, even at low CAV penetration rates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: To achieve these benefits while preserving CAV safety, existing research on mixed platoon control mainly relies on model-based control methods.
elements.sent-2411-020: These methods utilize microscopic car-following models, such as the intelligent driver model (IDM)~\cite{treiber2000congested and optimal velocity model (OVM)~\cite{bando1995dynamical , to capture the longitudinal behavior of HDVs.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Parametric models are then derived to represent the dynamics of the entire mixed platoon system, enabling the implementation of various model-based control strategies, including linear quadratic regulator~\cite{jin2016optimal , structured optimal control~\cite{wang2020controllability , model predictive control (MPC)~\cite{feng2021robust , $\mathcal{H _\infty$ robust control~\cite{wang2022robustness , and control barrier function~\cite{zhao2023safety .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, the inherent randomness and uncertainty in the car-following behavior of HDVs present a significant challenge in accurately identifying the mixed platoon dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-023: The resulting model mismatches may limit the performance of these model-based techniques.
elements.sent-2411-024: On the other hand, model-free or data-driven methods have gained increasing attention~\cite{gao2016data,huang2020learning,vinitsky2018lagrangian,lan2023safe .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Approaches like adaptive dynamic programming~\cite{gao2016data,huang2020learning and reinforcement learning~\cite{vinitsky2018lagrangian,wu2021flow have shown potential in learning CAV control policies through iterative training, without the necessity of a previous knowledge about the dynamics of mixed platoons.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, it is worth noting that safety is always prioritized first for CAVs, but these methods lack principled safety constraints, as they typically take an indirect manner by penalizing unsafe actions in the reward function.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although recent advancements, such as safe reinforcement learning, have begun to formally address safety concerns in mixed platoons~\cite{zhou2023safe,zhou2024enhancing , these methods still face significant challenges, including high computational demands and limited generalization capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-028: For deriving safe and optimal control inputs directly from data, one promising approach is data-driven predictive control, with the combination of the well-established MPC and data-driven techniques~\cite{hewing2020learning .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Along this direction, several methods have been proposed for data-driven mixed platoon control~\cite{lan2021data,wang2023deep,wu2023driver , with a notable example being Data-EnablEd Predictive Control (DeePC)~\cite{coulson2019data .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, DeePC represents the system behavior in a data-centric manner via Willems' fundamental lemma~\cite{willems2005note , and incorporates explicit input-output constraints in online predictive control optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-031: By adapting DeePC to a Leading Cruise Control (LCC) framework~\cite{wang2021leading , which is particularly designed for mixed traffic, the recently proposed Data-EnablEd Predictive Leading Cruise Control (\method{DeeP-LCC ) allows for CAVs' safe and optimal control in mixed platoons~\cite{wang2023deep .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The effectiveness of this approach has been validated across multiple dimensions, including the mitigation of traffic waves~\cite{wang2023deep , the reduction of energy consumption~\cite{li2024physics , and the enhancement of privacy protection~\cite{zhang2023privacy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, real-world data are always corrupted by noise from vehicle perception systems or V2X communication channels.
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, these data-driven CAV control systems become increasingly vulnerable to attacks in the V2X network, which may maliciously alter control inputs or perceived data to execute attacks~\cite{xu2022reachability , thereby compromising CAV control safety.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Existing research tends to overlook the influence of noise on data collection and online predictive control, and often assumes the absence of adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-036: This assumption could limit the CAV's ability to effectively follow desired trajectories and may raise significant safety concerns~\cite{zhao2023robust .}
elements.sent-2411-037: To explicitly address noise and attacks, growing evidence has indicated that robustness is crucial in standard DeePC~\cite{huang2023robust,berberich2020data .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Indeed, a recent paper has reformulated \method{DeeP-LCC using min-max robust optimization to handle unknown disturbances~\cite{shang2023smoothing .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, prior assumptions on disturbances are still needed to improve computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-040: Compared to the min-max approach, reachability analysis offers a more computationally reliable method for ensuring robustness against a wide range of noise and attacks.
elements.sent-2411-041: Several recent works have applied similar techniques to design robust control strategies for CAVs, including anti-attack control employing reach-avoid specification~\cite{xu2022reachability and formal safety net control using backward reachability analysis~\cite{schurmann2021formal .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Note that most of these methods are model-based, with one notable exception of~\cite{lan2021data , which presents a data-driven reachability analysis approach.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Nonetheless, the prediction accuracy in~\cite{lan2021data may be limited by the utilization of over-approximated data-driven dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Moreover, the boundaries of noise and attack have not been well-explored in~\cite{lan2021data , which could significantly affect the performance of data-driven predictive control.}
elements.sent-2411-045: To address the aforementioned research gaps, this paper proposes a Robust Data-EnablEd Predictive Leading Cruise Control (\method{RDeeP-LCC ) method that leverages data-driven reachability analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The goal is to develop robust data-driven control strategies for CAVs against noise and attacks in mixed platoons.
elements.sent-2411-047: We introduce specific evaluation indices to comprehensively analyze RDeeP-LCC's tracking performance under different boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-048: Additionally, human-in-the-loop experiments are conducted to provide near-real-world validation.
elements.sent-2411-049: Some preliminary results have been outlined in~\cite{li2024robust .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Precisely, the main contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-051: We propose a novel \method{RDeeP-LCC formulation for mixed platoon control that explicitly addresses process noise and adversarial attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Inspired by~\cite{lan2021data,alanwar2023data , we capture process noise and adversarial attacks as zonotope sets, in contrast to the zero assumption in~\cite{wang2023deep .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We decouple the system into nominal and error subsystems and use matrix zonotope set techniques to build an over-approximated error reachable set.
elements.sent-2411-054: By subtracting this set from the system constraints, we derive a tightened nominal reachable set, which is used as a safety constraint to reformulate the standard \method{DeeP-LCC problem.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The nominal control input is computed accordingly, and the actual control input for the CAV is obtained via a tube-based control manner, combining nominal and error feedback control inputs to enhance safety and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-056: We then perform numerical simulations to compare the control performance of \method{RDeeP-LCC with baseline methods, including standard MPC and standard \method{DeeP-LCC .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Specifically, our analysis focuses on quantifying the impact of varying noise and attack boundaries on platoon tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-058: The simulation results show that, without explicitly addressing noise and adversarial attacks, standard \method{DeeP-LCC performs even worse than traditional HDV-only traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-059: In contrast, the proposed \method{RDeeP-LCC consistently outperforms the baseline methods under various noise and attack boundary conditions, demonstrating its significant robustness improvements for data-driven control techniques in mixed traffic systems.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Finally, human-in-the-loop experiments are conducted with real human drivers engaged using driving simulators, whereas existing research primarily relies on simulations with HDVs represented by recorded data or car-following models.
elements.sent-2411-061: Results show that standard \method{DeeP-LCC fails to stabilize mixed traffic under noise and attack conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-062: By contrast, \method{RDeeP-LCC outperforms the baseline methods, achieving a $\SI{26.1 {\%}$ reduction in velocity deviations and a $\SI{24.7 {\%}$ decrease in real cost, compared to all-HDV traffic, while standard MPC exhibits smaller reductions of $\SI {18.8 {\% $ and $\SI {16.5 {\% $, respectively.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: These results highlight the superior robustness and practical effectiveness of \method{RDeeP-LCC in smoothing traffic flow against common noise and adversarial attacks.}

elements.sent-2411-067: Human-in-the-loop experiments are provided in Section~\ref{Sec:5 , and Section~\ref{Sec:6 concludes this paper.}}

elements.sent-2411-070: We consider a mixed platoon system comprising one leading CAV (indexed as $ 1 $) and $ n-1$ following HDVs (indexed as $ 2, \ldots,n$ against the moving direction), as shown in~\figurename~\ref{Fig:MixedPlatoon .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Define an index set $ \Omega =\{1, 2, \ldots, n\} $ encompassing the indices of all vehicles within the mixed platoon.
elements.sent-2411-072: The sets of CAV indices and HDV indices are denoted by $ \Omega_\mathrm{C =\{1\} $ and $ \Omega_\mathrm{H =\{2, \ldots, n\} $, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: All vehicles in the mixed platoon follow a head vehicle (HV) (indexed as $ 0 $), which is immediately ahead of the CAV.
elements.sent-2411-074: This mixed platoon system has been called the Leading Cruise Control (LCC) in~\cite{wang2021leading , and could be regarded as the minimum subsystem structure for the entire mixed traffic flow.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In this study, we consider a centralized control framework, which could be deployed in an edge cloud control platform.
elements.sent-2411-076: Precisely, we assume that roadside units can acquire state data for all the vehicles in the mixed platoon and transmit it to the cloud control platform without delay.
elements.sent-2411-077: Then, a specific controller within the cloud calculates the control commands, which are sent to the CAVs to regulate mixed platoons.
elements.sent-2411-078: It is worth noting that for platoon control, a small variation in the velocity of the head vehicle could necessitate a corresponding and synchronized adjustment in the velocity for all subsequent vehicles to maintain operational safety.
elements.sent-2411-079: Given the fact that only the CAV is under direct control in mixed platoons, the dynamic process of velocity adaptation poses substantial challenges, particularly in scenarios where the system is subject to process noise and adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-080: Under such conditions, the effective control of CAVs becomes crucial to ensure the safety and stability of the entire mixed platoon.
elements.sent-2411-081: Accordingly, the main research focus of this paper is to develop a robust data-driven control framework for CAVs that can effectively mitigate the impact of process noise and adversarial attacks.
elements.sent-2411-082: Particularly, as shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig:MixedPlatoon , we assume that the noise affects the observation process of the system state in the cloud control platform, while the attacks deceive or inject the control input received by the CAVs.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Parametric Model of Mixed Platoon System
elements.sent-2411-083: In the following, we introduce the parametric modeling process for mixed platoons, which is commonly used in model-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-084: For all vehicles $ i \in \Omega $, a second-order model is used to describe the longitudinal dynamics, given by~\cite{jin2016optimal,feng2021robust,wang2020controllability \begin{equation}\label{Eq:DynamicsModel} \begin{cases} \dot{p}_i(t) = v_i(t), \dot{v}_i(t) = u_i(t), \end{cases} \quad i \in \Omega\end{equation} where $p_i(t)$, $v_i(t)$, and $u_i(t)$ denote the position, velocity, and control input of the vehicle $i$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-085: For HDVs, the control input $u_i(t)$ in~\eqref{Eq:DynamicsModel is influenced by driver behavior, and several established car-following models, such as OVM~\cite{bando1995dynamical and IDM~\cite{treiber2000congested , provide specific formulations for this input.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Particularly, the general expression of $u_i(t)$ can be written as follows: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:HDVs_General} u_i(t) = F_{i}(s_i(t),\dot{s}_i(t),v_i(t)), \quad i \in \Omega_\mathrm{H},\end{equation} where $s_i(t) = p_{i-1 (t) - p_i(t)$ and $\dot{s _i(t) = v_{i-1 (t) - v_i(t)$ denote the spacing and relative velocity between vehicle $i$ and its preceding vehicle $i-1$, respectively, and $F_{i (\cdot)$ denotes the general car-following function.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Define spacing error $\tilde{s _i(t)$ and velocity error $\tilde{v _i(t)$ to indicate the deviations of spacing and velocity from their respective equilibrium states, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:Equilibrium} \begin{cases} \tilde{s}_i(t)={s}_i(t)-s^{*}_i, \tilde{v}_i(t)={v}_i(t)-v^{*}_i, \end{cases} \quad i \in \Omega\end{equation} where $s^*_i$ and $v^*_i$ represent the equilibrium spacing and velocity, which satisfy the condition $F(s^*_i,0,v^*_i) = 0$.}}

elements.figure-2411-002: {\includegraphics[width=18cm]{Figure//RDeePC.pdf}} \caption{Schematic of the proposed \method{RDeeP-LCC} method for mixed platoons. In the offline learning phase (blue), the method utilizes pre-collected data (yellow) to calculate the over-approximated system matrix set $\mathcal{M}_{ABHJ}$, derive a data-driven feedback control law $K$ to ensure stability for all possible systems, and generate the Hankel matrices. In the online control phase (green), the \method{RDeeP-LCC} solves for optimal control input for the CAV in a receding horizon strategy. Specifically, The system is decomposed into the error system and the nominal system. Using $\mathcal{M}_{ABHJ}$ and $K$, the method recursively derives the data-driven reachable set of error states, and then subtracts this set from the constraints of the original system to obtain a more compact nominal system constraint. Then, the nominal control input $u_\mathrm{z}(k)$ is calculated using the standard \method{DeeP-LCC} under the compact nominal constraint. Finally, the actual control input of the CAV is obtained by combining the nominal control input $u_\mathrm{z}(k)$ with the error feedback control input $u_\mathrm{e}(k)$ in a tube-based control manner.} \label{Fig:RDeeP}

elements.sent-2411-188: The final \method{RDeeP-LCC optimization problem for mixed platoon control is constructed as \begin{equation}\label{Eq:OptimizationProblemFinal} \begin{aligned} \min\limits_{g, u_\mathrm{z}, x_\mathrm{z},\sigma} \; J(x_\mathrm{z}, u_\mathrm{z}) + \lambda_{g}\|g\|_{2}^{2}+\lambda_{\sigma}\|\sigma\|_{2}^{2} \mathrm{ s.t. } \quad \eqref{Eq:NormalConstraint},~\eqref{Eq:DisturbanceConstraint},~\eqref{Eq:WilliemExpandingSlack}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{g}$ and $ \lambda_{\sigma $ denote the regularization penalty coefficients for the weighted two-norm for $ g $ and $\sigma $, respectively.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{\method{RDeeP-LCC}} \label{algorithm1} \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{Pre-collected data ($U$, $E$, $F$, $X$), constraints ($\mathcal{X}$, $\mathcal{U}$), weight ($Q$, $R$), bounded $\mathcal{Z}_{{\epsilon}}$, $\mathcal{Z}_{\vartheta}$, and $\mathcal{Z}_{\omega}$, past horizon $T_\mathrm{ini}$, control horizon $N$, total number of steps $N_f$.} Construct data ($U_-$, $E_-$, $F_-$, $X_-$, $X_+$) and data ($U_\mathrm{d}$, $E_\mathrm{d}$, $F_\mathrm{d}$, $X_\mathrm{d}$)\; Offline construct data Hankel matrices $ U_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ U_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ E_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ E_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ F_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ F_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ X_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ X_{\mathrm{f}} $ using~\eqref{Eq:Hankel}, the matrix zonotope set $\mathcal{M}_{ABHJ}$ using~\eqref{Eq:M_ABHJ}, and the feedback control law $K$ using~\eqref{Eq:ReachableSet} \; Initialize past mixed platoon data ($x_{\mathrm {ini }}$, $u_{\mathrm {ini}}$, ${\epsilon}_\mathrm{ini}$, ${\vartheta}_\mathrm{ini}$) at the initial time step $0$\; \While{$ 0 \leq k \leq N_f $}{ Compute data-driven error reachable sets to obtain $\hat{\mathcal{R}}_{k+i}^\mathrm{e}$ using~\eqref{Eq:ReachableSet}\; Compute constraint set for the nominal system to obtain $\mathcal{X}_\mathrm{z}(k+i)$ and $\mathcal{U}_\mathrm{z}(k+i)$ using~\eqref{Eq:RConstraint}\; Solve~\eqref{Eq:OptimizationProblemFinal} to obtain the optimal control input sequence and the state sequence for the nominal system $ u_\mathrm{z} = \mathrm{col}(u_\mathrm{z}(k), u_\mathrm{z}(k+1), \cdots, u_\mathrm{z}(k+N-1)) $ $ x_\mathrm{z} = \mathrm{col}(x_\mathrm{z}(k), x_\mathrm{z}(k+1), \cdots, x_\mathrm{z}(k+N-1)) $\; Measure the actual state $x(k)$ from the actual system\; Obtain $ u(k) $ from~\eqref{Eq:UFinal} and apply it as the control input to the CAV\; $ k \gets k+1 $ and update past mixed platoon data ($x_{\mathrm {ini }}$, $u_{\mathrm {ini }}$, ${\epsilon}_\mathrm{ini}$, ${\vartheta}_\mathrm{ini}$)\; }
elements.sent-2411-194: It is worth noting that in~\eqref{Eq:OptimizationProblemFinal , $x_{\mathrm {ini ,u_{\mathrm {ini ,\epsilon_{\mathrm {ini ,\vartheta_{\mathrm {ini }$ represent the past trajectories of the actual system~\eqref{Eq:DiscreteSystem , while $x_z, u_\mathrm{z ,\epsilon_\mathrm{z ,\vartheta_\mathrm{z}$ denote the predicted trajectories of the nominal system~\eqref{Eq:NominalDynamics .}}}}}}}}}}}}

In the offline learning phase (blue), the method utilizes pre-collected data (yellow) to calculate the over-approximated system matrix set $\mathcal{M}_{0.7{ABHJ}}$, derive a data-driven feedback control law $K$ to ensure stability for all possible systems, and generate the Hankel matrices. In the online control phase (green), the \method{RDeeP-LCC} solves for optimal control input for the CAV in a receding horizon strategy. Specifically, The system is decomposed into the error system and the nominal system. Finally, the actual control input of the CAV is obtained by combining the nominal control input $u_\mathrm{z}(k)$ with the error feedback control input $u_\mathrm{e}(k)$ in a tube-based control manner.} \label{Fig:RDeeP}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{\method{RDeeP-LCC}} \label{algorithm1} \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{Pre-collected data ($U$, $E$, $F$, $X$), constraints ($\mathcal{X}$, $\mathcal{U}$), weight ($Q$, $R$), bounded $\mathcal{Z}_{{\epsilon}}$, $\mathcal{Z}_{\vartheta}$, and $\mathcal{Z}_{\omega}$, past horizon $T_\mathrm{ini}$, control horizon $N$, total number of steps $N_f$.} Construct data ($U_-$, $E_-$, $F_-$, $X_-$, $X_+$) and data ($U_\mathrm{d}$, $E_\mathrm{d}$, $F_\mathrm{d}$, $X_\mathrm{d}$)\; Offline construct Hankel matrices $ U_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ U_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ E_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ E_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ F_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ F_{\mathrm{f}} $, $ X_{\mathrm{p}} $, $ X_{\mathrm{f}} $ using~\eqref{Eq:Hankel}, the matrix zonotope set $\mathcal{M}_{0.7{ABHJ}}$ using~\eqref{Eq:M_ABHJ}, and the feedback control law $K$ using~\eqref{Eq:ReachableSet} \; Initialize past mixed platoon data ($x_{\mathrm {ini }}$, $u_{\mathrm {ini}}$, ${\epsilon}_\mathrm{ini}$, ${\vartheta}_\mathrm{ini}$) at the initial time step $0$\; \While{$ 0 \leq k \leq N_f $}{ Compute data-driven error reachable sets to obtain $\hat{\mathcal{R}}_{k+i}^\mathrm{e}$ using~\eqref{Eq:ReachableSet}\; Compute constraint set for the nominal system to obtain $\mathcal{X}_\mathrm{z}(k+i)$ and $\mathcal{U}_\mathrm{z}(k+i)$ using~\eqref{Eq:RConstraint}\; Solve~\eqref{Eq:OptimizationProblemFinal} to obtain the optimal control input sequence $u_\mathrm{z}$ and the state sequence $x_\mathrm{z}$ for the nominal system \; Measure the actual state $x(k)$ from the actual system\; Obtain $ u(k) $ from~\eqref{Eq:UFinal} and apply it as the control input to the CAV\; $ k \gets k+1 $ and update past mixed platoon data ($x_{\mathrm {ini }}$, $u_{\mathrm {ini }}$, ${\epsilon}_\mathrm{ini}$, ${\vartheta}_\mathrm{ini}$)\; }
